elements.sent-2411-001: Pre-trained vision-language models provide a robust foundation for efficient transfer learning across various downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-002: In the field of video action recognition, mainstream approaches often introduce additional parameter modules to capture temporal information.
elements.sent-2411-003: While the increased model capacity brought by these additional parameters helps better fit the video-specific inductive biases, existing methods require learning a large number of parameters and are prone to catastrophic forgetting of the original generalizable knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective Multi-modal Spatio-Temporal Adapter (MSTA) to improve the alignment between representations in the text and vision branches, achieving a balance between general knowledge and task-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, to mitigate over-fitting and enhance generalizability, we introduce a spatio-temporal description-guided consistency constraint.
elements.sent-2411-006: This constraint involves feeding template inputs (i.e., ``a video of $\{cls\}$'') into the trainable language branch, while LLM-generated spatio-temporal descriptions are input into the pre-trained language branch, enforcing consistency between the outputs of the two branches.
elements.sent-2411-007: This mechanism prevents over-fitting to downstream tasks and improves the distinguishability of the trainable branch within the spatio-temporal semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-008: We evaluate the effectiveness of our approach across four tasks: zero-shot transfer, few-shot learning, base-to-novel generalization, and fully-supervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-009: Compared to many state-of-the-art methods, our MSTA achieves outstanding performance across all evaluations, while using only 2-7\% of the trainable parameters in the original model.

elements.sent-2411-042: The key contributions of this work are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-043: We propose a novel Multi-modal Spatio-Temporal Adapter (MSTA) that contains separate projection layers to improve feature representations for video and text encoders independently.

elements.sent-2411-045: We propose a spatio-temporal description-guided consistency constraint for large multi-modal foundation models, enabling them to learn new tasks from a small number of samples while maintaining their generalization capability.
elements.sent-2411-046: Extensive experiments demonstrate that MSTA achieves an optimal balance between new and old knowledge while training only a small number of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-047: Comprehensive ablation studies showcase the scalability and effectiveness of our proposed method.

elements.sent-2411-075: ViCLIP is composed of two branches: a text branch with encoder $E_t$ and a vision branch with encoder $E_v$.

elements.sent-2411-077: In particular, a video $V \in \mathbb{R ^{T\times H\times W\times3}$ will be fed into the encoder $E_v$ to obtain the video feature $x$ as follows: \begin{equation}x_0 = PatchEmbed(V), \label{visualembed} [c_i,x_i] = E^i_v([c_{i-1}, x_{i-1}]),\,i=1,2,...,L,\label{each_visual_layer} x = PatchProj(c_L).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Here, $\text{PatchEmbed}$ first divides the input video $V$ into fixed-size patches, projecting them into feature embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-079: A learnable class token $c_0$ is then concatenated with these embeddings, forming $[c_0, x_0]$, which is subsequently passed through $L$ transformer blocks, represented as $\{\mathcal{E ^i_v\}_{i=1 ^{L}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Finally, the class token $ c_L$ from the last transformer block $\mathcal{E ^L_v$ is projected into the video feature $ x $ via a projection layer $\text{PatchProj}$, positioning the feature in the shared vision-language space.}
elements.sent-2411-081: In a similar manner, a text description $T$ is processed through the text encoder $E_t$ to obtain the text feature $w$ as follows: \begin{equation}w_0 = TextEmbed(T), w_i = E^i_t(w_{i-1}),\, i=1,2,...,L,\label{each_text_layer} w = TextProj(w_L).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}w_0 = TextEmbed(T), w_i = E^i_t(w_{i-1}),\, i=1,2,...,L,\label{each_text_layer} w = TextProj(w_L).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-082: As shown, this process involves three steps: first, $\text{TextEmbed}$ is used to tokenize and project the input text description into the initial word embedding $w_0$.
elements.sent-2411-083: Next, a series of transformer blocks $ \{\mathcal{E _t^i\}_{i=1 ^{L}$ progressively abstracts the features, producing a refined embedding $ w_L $ at the final layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-084: Finally, $\text{TextProj}$ projects the output $ w_L $ from the last transformer block $ \mathcal{E _t^L$ into the common vision-language space.}

elements.sent-2411-112: To address the issue of decreased generalization ability due to over-fitting on downstream tasks, we propose the Spatio-Temporal Description-guided Consistency constraint.
elements.sent-2411-113: This constraint ensures that the embeddings generated by the trainable model (adjustable adapter parameters in the image and text branches) do not significantly deviate from those generated by the pre-trained multi-modal foundation model, while enhancing the understanding of temporal and spatial features.
elements.sent-2411-114: In the language branch, we leverage a pre-trained large language model (LLM)~\cite{DeepSeek-V2 to generate more descriptive sentences that better capture the temporal and spatio aspects of actions.}
elements.sent-2411-115: The spatio description aims to capture static visual elements that can be discerned from a single image, such as scenes and common objects.
elements.sent-2411-116: The temporal description, on the other hand, is designed to decompose action categories in a step-by-step manner, describing the temporal evolution of actions.
elements.sent-2411-117: To generate spatio descriptions, we use the following prompt with category name $\{cls\}$ to query LLM: \textcolor{gray {``Please give me N sentences describing the visual appearance of the action $\{cls\$." .}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: For temporal descriptions, we utilize the the following temporal prompt: \textcolor{gray {``Please give me N sentences describing the temporally decoupled steps of the action $\{cls\$." .}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: Through the above operations, we obtain the spatio descriptions $\text{DES _s$ and temporal $\text{DES _t$, each containing $N$ descriptions.}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Subsequently, we feed sentences based on standard template (i.e., a video of $\{cls\}$.) into the learnable branch, while spatio/temporal descriptions are provided to the pre-trained branch.
elements.sent-2411-121: We employ cosine distance as a consistency constraint between the embeddings from the pre-trained branch and the learnable branch.
elements.sent-2411-122: The pre-trained encoder extracts features from the spatio/temporal descriptions, allowing the consistency constraint to be defined as: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\rm CC} = 2 - \frac{w^c\cdot D^c_s}{||w^c\cdot D^c_t||||D^c_s||} - \frac{w^c}{||w^c||||D^c_t||},\end{equation} where $w^c$ indicates the text embedding for class $c$ of the pre-trained branch, and $D^c_s$ represents the mean embedding of the spatio descriptions for class $c$, obtained by averaging the features of the input descriptions processed through the pre-trained branch.
elements.sent-2411-123: Similarly, $D^c_t$ is computed for the temporal descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-124: This consistency constraint loss is combined with a supervised loss to create the final loss function.
elements.sent-2411-125: The supervised loss is given by: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\rm CE} = -log \frac{{\rm exp}(sim(x, w^c)/\tau}{\sum_{k=1}^C{\rm exp}(sim(x, w^k)/\tau},\end{equation} where $\tau$ is a temperature parameter controlling the sharpness of similarity scores.
elements.sent-2411-126: By combining these losses with a weighting factor $\alpha$, the final loss function for MSTA becomes: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{\rm CE} + \alpha\mathcal{L}_{\rm CC}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-171: We conduct ablation studies on base-to-novel settings in Table~\ref{ablation to investigate the learning capacity and generalizability of our model in different instantiations.}
elements.sent-2411-173: We began by examining the impact of various adapter configurations.
elements.sent-2411-174: Specifically, we compared the performance of uni-modal adapters, which are adapters applied exclusively to either the vision (V-) or language (L-) branch, against the performance of our full MSTA model, which incorporates adapters in both modalities.
elements.sent-2411-175: Our findings, as presented in Table 4a, clearly indicate that the uni-modal adapters are outperformed by the full MSTA model.
elements.sent-2411-176: This suggests that the integration of both visual and textual information is crucial for optimal performance.
elements.sent-2411-177: Moreover, we discovered that omitting the shared projection layers (w/o Shared Layers) results in a noticeable performance drop.
elements.sent-2411-178: When these shared layers are included, we observe an increase in the harmonic mean (HM) from 59.5 to 60.1 across the four datasets.
elements.sent-2411-180: The dimension of the shared layer in our MSTA determines the number of parameters required to extract relationships from the features of the two modalities.
elements.sent-2411-181: We conducted an ablation study by systematically varying the dimension of the shared layer to assess its impact.
elements.sent-2411-182: As shown in Table~\ref{dimension , accuracy for base categories peaks as the intermediate dimension increases, while accuracy for novel categories saturates around 256.}
elements.sent-2411-183: This may be due to larger dimensions introducing more trainable parameters, which increases the risk of over-fitting.. Scaling Factor $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-184: The scaling factor $\lambda$ is a critical hyperparameter that controls the balance between general and task-specific features within the MSTA.
elements.sent-2411-185: As shown in Table~\ref{s_factor , we thoroughly evaluated various scaling factors.}
elements.sent-2411-186: Our results reveal that a scaling factor of $\lambda = 0.005$ provides the best performance trade-off (HM) between base and novel categories.
elements.sent-2411-187: This optimal balance is essential for enabling the model to quickly adapt to base categories without sacrificing its ability to generalize to novel categories.
elements.sent-2411-188: In contrast, larger scaling factors, while improving performance on base categories, can hinder the model's performance on novel categories.
elements.sent-2411-189: Smaller scaling factors, on the other hand, can limit the model's adaptability to downstream tasks.. Weighting Factor $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-190: The weighting factor $\alpha$ associated with the consistency constraint loss is a key hyperparameter that influences model performance.
elements.sent-2411-191: As shown in Table~\ref{w_factor , the choice of $\alpha$ has a significant impact on accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-192: The model achieves peak performance when $\alpha = 1.0$.
elements.sent-2411-193: Deviations from this value, whether by increasing or decreasing $\alpha$, lead to a decline in performance.
elements.sent-2411-194: This finding underscores the importance of carefully tuning the consistency constraint to maintain a balance between adaptability and generalization capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-196: We evaluate the impact of adding MSTA to different encoder layers, with results summarized in Table~\ref{inserted .}
elements.sent-2411-197: The performance of MSTA improves consistently as the number of added layers increases.
elements.sent-2411-198: Furthermore, given the same number of layers, MSTA performs better when placed higher in the network (i.e., farther from the input).
elements.sent-2411-199: For example, inserting MSTA into layers 7 to 12 yields 0.3 higher HM than inserting into layers 1 to 6, despite both configurations using six MSTA layers.
elements.sent-2411-200: Notably, the insertion of MSTA in layers 8 to 12 delivers the best performance for base class tasks, which is particularly advantageous for few-shot learning scenarios where rapid adaptation to new categories is essential.

elements.sent-2411-203: The results presented in Table~\ref{description show that each description complements the others, highlighting the importance of both spatiotemporal descriptions for recognition tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-204: Additionally, we observe that the temporal descriptions have a more pronounced effect on performance compared to the spatiotemporal descriptions.

elements.sent-2411-208: In conclusion, this paper presents a novel Multi-modal Spatio-Temporal Adapter (MSTA) for efficient transfer learning in video-language foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-209: The MSTA is designed to enhance the alignment between text and video representations, thereby achieving a balance between general knowledge and task-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-210: Our method introduces a spatio-temporal description-guided consistency constraint to mitigate over-fitting and enhance generalizability, which is particularly beneficial in data limited scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-211: Through extensive experiments across various tasks, our MSTA demonstrates outstanding performance and achieves state-of-the-art results while using a minimal number of trainable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-212: The comprehensive ablation studies further validate the effectiveness and scalability of our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-213: Overall, our work contributes significantly to the field of video action recognition by providing an efficient and effective transfer learning approach that preserves the generalizability of pre-trained models while adapting them to specific downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-214: Although our method achieves excellent results across various video recognition settings, there are still areas for improvement.
elements.sent-2411-215: For example, MSTA connects the gradients of visual and textual features.
elements.sent-2411-216: Despite using gradient scaling to prevent instability, we believe that the backpropagation performed under this setup is not optimal.
elements.sent-2411-217: Therefore, exploring better ways to train this type of module could further enhance MSTA's performance.
elements.sent-2411-218: On the other hand, using spatiotemporal descriptions generated by LLMs for constraints may lead to performance degradation due to the lower quality of these descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-219: In future work, we will further investigate how to leverage LLMs to improve the efficient transfer of video-language models, aiming to explore the generality and versatility of our approach.. Broader Impact.
elements.sent-2411-220: The adaptability of foundational models to various downstream tasks has become a major focus in the current field of machine learning~\cite{LoRA,AdaptFormer,ST-Adapter .}
elements.sent-2411-221: We believe that retaining the existing capabilities of foundational models while integrating new knowledge is an important issue worth exploring in depth.
elements.sent-2411-222: We hope this research provides new insights and approaches for the broader and long-term application of foundational models.
elements.sent-2411-223: Our work focuses on video recognition tasks, which have widespread applications in real-world scenarios such as surveillance.
elements.sent-2411-224: However, before these technologies can be deployed, it is crucial to carefully address potential issues related to privacy protection and rights.
elements.sent-2411-225: The pseudo-code implementation of MSTA is provided below, along with a concise definition of MSTA and its insertion method.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Implementation of Text Adapter \& Shared Adapter in PyTorch-like style} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE \textbf{\textcolor{purple}{def}} \textcolor{teal}{\_build\_adapter}(d\_model, n\_layers, l\_start, l\_end, mid\_dim, dropout\_rate=0.1): \STATE adapter = [None] * (n\_layers + 1) \STATE for i in range(l\_start, l\_end+1): \STATE if mid\_dim == d\_model: \STATE adapter[i] = nn.Sequential( \STATE nn.Linear(d\_model, mid\_dim), \STATE nn.ReLU() \STATE ) \STATE else: \STATE adapter[i] = nn.Sequential(OrderedDict([ \STATE ("down", nn.Sequential( \STATE nn.Linear(d\_model, mid\_dim), \STATE nn.ReLU() \STATE )), \STATE ("dropout", nn.Dropout(p=dropout\_rate)), \STATE ("up", nn.Linear(mid\_dim, d\_model)) \STATE ])) \STATE adapter = nn.ModuleList([a for a in adapter]) \STATE \textbf{\textcolor{purple}{return}} adapter \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Implementation of Visual Adapter in PyTorch-like style} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE \textbf{\textcolor{purple}{def}} \textcolor{teal}{\_build\_visual\_adapter}(d\_model, n\_layers, l\_start, l\_end, mid\_dim, dropout\_rate=0.1): \STATE adapter = [None] * (n\_layers + 1) \STATE for i in range(l\_start, l\_end+1): \STATE adapter[i] = nn.Sequential(OrderedDict([ \STATE ("down", nn.Sequential( \STATE nn.Linear(d\_model, mid\_dim), \STATE nn.ReLU() \STATE )), \STATE ("dropout", nn.Dropout(p=dropout\_rate)), \STATE ("up", nn.Linear(mid\_dim, d\_model)), \STATE ("temporal\_up", nn.Conv3d(mid\_dim, d\_model, kernel\_size=(3,1,1), stride=(1, 1, 1), \STATE padding="same", groups=mid\_dim, dilation=(1, 1, 1))), \STATE ])) \STATE adapter = nn.ModuleList([a for a in adapter]) \STATE \textbf{\textcolor{purple}{return}} adapter \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-226: In the field of video action recognition, mainstream approaches often introduce additional modules to capture temporal information.
elements.sent-2411-227: Although the additional modules increase the capacity of model, enabling it to better capture video-specific inductive biases, existing methods typically introduce a substantial number of new parameters and are prone to catastrophic forgetting of previously acquired generalizable knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-228: In this paper, we propose a parameter-efficient Multi-modal Spatio-Temporal Adapter (MSTA) to enhance the alignment between textual and visual representations, achieving a balance between generalizable knowledge and task-specific adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-229: This constraint involves providing template inputs (e.g., "a video of \{cls\}") to the trainable language branch and LLM-generated spatio-temporal descriptions to the pre-trained language branch, enforcing output consistency between the branches.
elements.sent-2411-230: This approach reduces overfitting to downstream tasks and enhances the distinguishability of the trainable branch within the spatio-temporal semantic space.

elements.sent-2411-234: In video action recognition, this need is particularly evident due to the dynamic and temporal nature of video data, requiring models to effectively capture context and temporal relationships.
elements.sent-2411-235: Consequently, existing methods typically enhance pre-trained CLIP models by integrating carefully designed prompts~\cite{Vita-CLIP , adapters~\cite{MoTE,ST-Adapter,AIM , or temporal modules~\cite{XCLIP to better acquire video-specific knowledge.}}}
elements.sent-2411-236: For instance, XCLIP~\cite{XCLIP employs cross-frame attention and multi-frame integration modules to model temporal information.}
elements.sent-2411-237: ActionCLIP~\cite{ActionCLIP uses pre-training to generate robust representations, applying prompt engineering to align the action recognition task closely with the pre-trained model, followed by fine-tuning to achieve superior performance.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Despite the increased model capacity resulting from numerous learnable parameters, such approaches can lead to catastrophic forgetting, thereby diminishing the generalizability of the original models.
elements.sent-2411-239: Alternatively, Wang et al.~\cite{InternVid proposed ViCLIP to enhance CLIP performance specifically for video understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-240: ViCLIP is trained on extensive video-text pairs and incorporates spatiotemporal attention within the visual encoder, retaining other CLIP components unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-241: However, current research has not yet explored effective transfer learning methods specifically designed for ViCLIP, and existing CLIP-based methods cannot be directly applied.
elements.sent-2411-242: Thus, the critical question arises: Is there an efficient fine-tuning method for pre-trained models that preserves generalizability? One possible approach is directly applying efficient transfer methods used for image/text foundation models, such as LoRA~\cite{LoRA and AdaptFormer~\cite{AdaptFormer , to video tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-243: However, these methods primarily rely on uni-modal information, making them unsuitable for multi-modal models.
elements.sent-2411-244: Another simple strategy involves independently applying adapters to each modality, but this overlooks the interrelation between text and video representations.
elements.sent-2411-245: Consequently, directly applying identical adapters may not effectively capture task-specific nuances that vary across vision and language modalities.
elements.sent-2411-246: Furthermore, existing approaches do not fully address the distinct characteristics inherent in text and video representations.
elements.sent-2411-247: Effective transfer learning typically involves balancing discrimination and generalization—fine-tuning task-specific discriminative features while preserving features broadly applicable across different tasks.
elements.sent-2411-248: As shown in Fig.~\ref{tunable_params , our method achieves the best generalization performance with a minimal number of parameters.}
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[width=0.99\linewidth]{MMA.pdf} \caption{The proposed Multi-modal Spatio-Temporal Adapter (MSTA) is designed for transformer-based multi-modal foundation models. It optimizes only the additional adapters, keeping the pretrained CLIP model frozen. To balance discrimination and generalization, we selectively fine-tune a few higher layers ($\geq k$). MSTA employs shared weights for video and text representations, enabling the model to capture shared cues from both modalities.} \label{MAIN_ARC}
elements.sent-2411-249: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{MAIN_ARC , unlike most existing methods that introduce adapters or tokens across the entire network or into lower layers, our adapter $\mathcal{A}$ is selectively incorporated into only a few higher layers of both the video and text encoders.}

elements.sent-2411-269: In conclusion, this paper introduces a novel Multi-modal Spatio-Temporal Adapter (MSTA) designed for efficient transfer learning in video-language foundation models.
elements.sent-2411-270: The MSTA aims to enhance alignment between text and video representations, achieving an optimal balance between general knowledge and task-specific knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-271: The proposed method incorporates a spatio-temporal description-guided consistency constraint to reduce overfitting and improve generalization, particularly beneficial in scenarios with limited data.
elements.sent-2411-272: Extensive experiments conducted on diverse tasks demonstrate outstanding performance and state-of-the-art results, achieved with minimal trainable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-273: Comprehensive ablation studies further confirm the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed approach.
elements.sent-2411-274: Overall, our work significantly advances the field of video action recognition by offering an efficient transfer learning framework that maintains the generalizability of pre-trained models while effectively adapting them to specific downstream tasks.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
